OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 75-year-old male patient with a history of non-metastatic urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer, both successfully treated surgically two years ago. During follow-up, a palpable, hard, and fixed cervical mass was detected. No history of neck trauma or radiation.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound showed a 6 cm solid and heterogeneous left mass with internal calcifications and intralesional vascularisation, not arising from the thyroid gland (Figures 1a and 1b). This mass was displacing and compressing the left internal jugular vein, which was still permeable (Figures 1c, 1d, and 1e). Findings were consistent with a calcified adenopathy.
 
 A subsequent non-contrast CT due to renal impairment revealed a solid left supraclavicular mass, with a heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, and thick calcifications. Thickening of the lateral-cervical ipsilateral musculature was also observed (Figures 2a and 2b). There was a pathological adenopathy with calcifications at the Va cervical level (Figure 2c).
 
 A core needle biopsy was performed, and histology reported infiltration of spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, and atypical cells, followed by the formation of an osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue (Figures 3a and 3b).

This mass was displacing and compressing the left internal jugular vein, which was still permeable. Findings were consistent with a calcified adenopathy. A subsequent non-contrast CT due to renal impairment revealed a solid left supraclavicular mass, with a heterogeneous density, irregular outlines, and thick calcifications. Thickening of the lateral-cervical ipsilateral musculature was also observed. There was a pathological adenopathy with calcifications at the Va cervical level. A core needle biopsy was performed, and histology reported infiltration of spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, and atypical cells, followed by the formation of an osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 75-year-old male, history of non-metastatic urothelial carcinoma and prostate cancer (both treated surgically two years prior).  
- Clinical: New palpable, hard, fixed cervical mass.  
- Ultrasound: 6 cm heterogeneous left mass, solid, with internal calcifications and vascularization, not thyroid-derived, displacing/compressing left internal jugular vein (still patent), consistent with calcified lymphadenopathy.  
- CT: Solid left supraclavicular mass, heterogeneous density, irregular contours, thick calcifications; thickened ipsilateral lateral cervical musculature; pathological calcified lymph node at level Va.  
- Histology: Core needle biopsy shows spindle-shaped, pleomorphic, atypical cells with formation of osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue.  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Extraskeletal osteosarcoma:  
   - Pros: Histology showing spindle-shaped pleomorphic atypical cells forming osteoid matrix and chondroid tissue strongly supports osteosarcoma; location in soft tissue (supraclavicular mass) and not attached to bone (extraskeletal); aggressive features with internal calcifications consistent with matrix production.  
   - Cons: Relatively rare, but fit with histology and imaging.  
2) Myositis ossificans:  
   - Pros: Can show calcifications and muscle involvement; usually following trauma (none here).  
   - Cons: Typically benign and zonal pattern on imaging/histology; no spindle cells or osteoid matrix formation from malignant cells; patient has no trauma history; lesion is large, invasive, and with pleomorphic cells.  
3) Calcified metastatic lymphadenopathy:  
   - Pros: Patient has history of malignancy, calcified lymph nodes possible in metastases (e.g., from papillary thyroid carcinoma, but no thyroid origin here).  
   - Cons: Histology typically shows malignant epithelial cells, not spindle-shaped pleomorphic cells with osteoid/chondroid matrix; mass is not thyroid-related; osteoid/chondroid matrix formation is not typical in metastatic carcinoma to lymph nodes.  
4) Benign conditions with calcified lymph nodes (e.g., tuberculosis, sarcoidosis, amyloidosis):  
   - Pros: Can cause calcified nodes; no trauma or radiation.  
   - Cons: These are benign inflammatory conditions, histology does not match; no granulomas or amyloid seen; presence of atypical spindle cells with osteoid/chondroid matrix formation does not fit; lesion aggressive and large.  
5) Another type of high-grade sarcoma:  
   - Pros: Could be sarcoma with calcifications; soft tissue location.  
   - Cons: Histology specifically shows osteoid and chondroid matrix, which is characteristic of osteosarcoma rather than other sarcomas typically lacking bone/cartilage matrix formation.  

C) Ranking and tie-break:  
1) Extraskeletal osteosarcoma (strong histologic evidence of osteoid/chondroid matrix with pleomorphic spindle cells; location consistent; imaging supports aggressive soft tissue mass with calcifications).  
2) Another type of high-grade sarcoma (possible but less specific due to matrix formation).  
3) Calcified metastatic lymphadenopathy (less likely due to histology mismatch).  
4) Benign calcified lymph node conditions (unlikely given malignant histology).  
5) Myositis ossificans (least likely due to lack of trauma and malignant histology).  

Thus, the best diagnosis is extraskeletal osteosarcoma based on histology (osteoid and chondroid matrix formation by malignant spindle cells) and imaging findings.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Extraskeletal osteosarcoma  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A sixteen-year-old male patient presented with congenital deafness on the left side and normal hearing on the right side. There was a family history of deafness and hearing loss on the father’s side. He experienced relatively few difficulties in daily life. Audiometry showed near-total deafness on the left side and normal thresholds on the right.
 Imaging Findings: A 3T MR study of the temporal bones was performed. The thin, heavily T2-weighted images revealed a bud-like, cystic cochlea on the left side with absent normal turns, modiolus and interscalar septa (Figures 1a, 1b, 1c and 6). Additionally, there was no identifiable left cochlear nerve, and apart from the facial nerve, there was only one other nerve, smaller in calibre compared to the facial nerve, in the internal auditory canal, consistent with a hypoplastic vestibulocochlear nerve and cochlear nerve aplasia (Figures 3a, 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f). The vestibular nerve did not bifurcate into a superior and inferior branch. The left inner ear canal was small in calibre as well, progressively narrowing from medial to lateral. The left vestibule appeared mildly globose (Figure 2). The left lateral semicircular canal exhibited focal signal loss, which could either be a true abnormality or an artefact. The inner ear structures and inner ear canal on the right side were normally developed (Figures 1a, 1b, 3b, 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, 5e, and 5f).

FinalDiagnosis: Cochlear hypoplasia type I (with aplasia of the cochlear nerve)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old male with a history of recurrent urolithiasis presented with persistent abdominal pain and unexplained fever. Blood tests revealed elevated inflammatory markers and impaired renal function.
 Imaging Findings: On the CT scan, the left kidney is markedly enlarged, and multiple kidney calculi, including a staghorn calculus, are identified within the calyces and renal pelvis (Figures 1a and 2a). Diffuse thinning of the renal cortex and non-homogeneous, poorly differentiated renal parenchyma, replaced by multiple hypodense fluid-filled cavities (mean attenuation ~ +11 HU) (Figure 1b), with rim enhancement (Figure 3), consistent with the “bear paw sign”, associated with minimal air-fluid levels. Fat-density areas (mean attenuation ~ -60 HU) within and adjacent to the fluid collections (xanthomas) are seen (Figure 1c). The renal pelvis and calyces are dilated. The perirenal fat stranding and thickening of both Gerota’s and Zuckerkandl’s fasciae are noted, indicating stage II–III inflammatory extension. The entire ureter shows diffuse wall thickening with fat stranding and linear calcifications in the mid-ureter (Figure 2b). On the delayed urographic phase, there is no contrast excretion, confirming impaired left kidney function (Figure 4a and 4b).

1. Renal carcinoma  
   - Pros: Enlarged kidney, irregular parenchyma could suggest neoplasm.  
   - Cons: Presence of staghorn calculus and typical "bear paw sign," fat-density xanthomas, and extensive perirenal inflammation favor inflammatory/infectious process over malignancy. Rim-enhancing cavities with fluid and fat-density areas are not typical for carcinoma.

   - Pros: Rim-enhancing fluid collections, systemic signs of infection and fever, inflammatory changes.  
   - Cons: Abscess usually presents as a single or few fluid collections, not diffuse multiple hypodense cavities replaced kidney parenchyma, plus fat-density xanthomas are uncommon in simple abscess.    - Pros: Classic "bear paw sign" on CT with multiple hypodense fluid collections replacing renal parenchyma, presence of staghorn calculus, fat-density xanthomas ("fatty infiltration or xanthomas"), marked enlargement of kidney, ureteral involvement with fat stranding and thickening, perirenal fat stranding, no contrast excretion, inflammatory signs.    - Cons: None significant.

4. Malakoplakia - very rare and no imaging features stated.  
5. Renal carcinoma - possible but imaging features more inflammatory.  
6. Lymphoma - less likely given imaging findings.  

</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 29-year-old female patient presented to the cardiology department with episodes of numbness in both her upper and lower extremities, which spontaneously resolved after 30 minutes. She also reported episodes of chest pain that she attributed to stress. Heart auscultation was normal. ECG showed diffuse ST-elevations.
 Imaging Findings: Echocardiography (ECG) demonstrated severe systolic heart failure, with a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) of 30%. The exam showed basal hypokinesia and preserved apical contractility, along with irregularities in the lining of the left ventricular myocardium (Figure 1).
 
 A contrast-enhanced computed tomography (CECT) scan revealed no coronary abnormalities but confirmed the irregularities in the left ventricle wall (Figure 2).
 
 Subsequently, a cardiac MRI was performed, which revealed moderate left ventricle dilation and irregularities in the ventricle wall, including protrusions of myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, and thinning of the inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia (Figures 3 and 4). Severe left ventricular dysfunction was confirmed, with an LVEF of 20% and a global longitudinal strain (GLS) of -7.85%, as well as ventricular dyssynchrony. A non-ischemic fibrosis region was also present in the interventricular septum and the posterior hinge point. The right ventricle appeared to be normal in morphology and function.

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- 29-year-old female with transient numbness and stress-related chest pain.
- ECG: diffuse ST elevations.
- Echocardiography: severe systolic dysfunction (LVEF 30%), basal hypokinesia with preserved apical contractility, irregularities in left ventricular myocardium lining.
- CECT: no coronary artery abnormalities, confirms LV wall irregularities.
- Cardiac MRI: moderate LV dilation, irregularities including myocardial bridge protrusions, multiple crypts, thinning of inferolateral apex with diffuse dyskinesia.
- Severe LV dysfunction (LVEF 20%, GLS -7.85%), ventricular dyssynchrony.
- Non-ischemic fibrosis in interventricular septum and posterior hinge point.
- RV morphology and function normal.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Left ventricular noncompaction (LVNC):
  - Pros: LV dilation, irregularities, presence of crypts.
  - Cons: Noncompaction typically shows prominent trabeculations with deep intertrabecular recesses and a two-layered myocardium (noncompacted to compacted ratio >2.3). Basal hypokinesia and preserved apical contractility can be seen, but myocardial bridge protrusions and fibrosis in septum and hinge point are less typical. No mention of the classic noncompaction findings in MRI.

2) Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy (STCM):
  - Pros: Wall irregularities with protruding myocardial bridges, multiple crypts, basal hypokinesis with apical sparing, severe LV dysfunction, non-ischemic fibrosis in septum and hinge points. Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy is a recently described entity characterized by deep saw-tooth-like invaginations or crypts in the LV wall, often with fibrosis, systolic dysfunction, and can be associated with genetic cardiomyopathies.
  - Cons: Very rare and not widely known; however, given the imaging findings, it fits well.

3) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM):
  - Pros: Myocardial fibrosis in septum and hinge points, possible crypts.
  - Cons: No mention of hypertrophy or increased wall thickness; instead, thinning of the inferolateral apex and baseline hypokinesia with preserved apical contractility (inverse of typical HCM), and LV dilation rather than hypertrophy argue against HCM.

4) Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (ACM):
  - Pros: LV fibrosis and wall irregularities could be consistent.
  - Cons: Typically involves fibro-fatty infiltration, especially in the RV or LV, often with aneurysms; RV is normal here; no fatty infiltration reported; LV involvement without fatty infiltration less typical; crypts and myocardial bridges not classic.

C) Ranking:
1) Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy (most consistent with myocardial bridge protrusions, crypts, LV dysfunction, non-ischemic fibrosis in typical locations).
2) Left ventricular noncompaction (some overlap with crypts and LV dilation, but lacks definitive noncompaction criteria).
3) Arrhythmogenic cardiomyopathy (less likely due to normal RV and lack of fatty infiltration).
4) Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (least likely due to absence of hypertrophy and presence of thinning/apical hypokinesia).

Given the pathognomonic finding of "protrusions of myocardial bridges" and multiple crypts with fibrosis, the diagnosis is best matched to Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy.

</analysis><final> Saw-tooth cardiomyopathy </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-month-old infant presented with a history of on-and-off soft tissue swelling over the left cheek since 2 months (Figure 1). No history of trauma. Full-term normal delivery.
 Imaging Findings: Head and neck CT with bone reconstruction showed cortical irregularity with sclerosis, solid periosteal reaction, increase in cortical width and density, seen along the angle and ramus of the left hemimandible (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 On MRI, the corresponding area showed mild STIR hyperintensity (Figure 3) and T1 hypointensity (Figure 4). No evidence of any significant diffusion restriction was seen in this region (Figures 5a and 5b). Surrounding oedematous changes were seen in the left masticator muscles and infratemporal fossa. No focal discrete collection or solid component was seen.

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Osteomyelitis  
   - Pros: Periosteal reaction, cortical irregularity, surrounding edema can be seen in osteomyelitis; soft tissue swelling.

2) Battered baby syndrome  
   - Pros: Periosteal reaction and cortical irregularity could be trauma.  
   - Cons: No history of trauma; lesions are well-defined cortical changes with solid periosteal reaction, not typical fracture or healing fractures; swelling intermittent, not acute trauma; no other injury signs.  
3) Hypervitaminosis A  
   - Pros: Can cause periosteal reaction and cortical sclerosis.  
   - Cons: Usually more diffuse periosteal thickening; infant likely no history of vitamin A excess; no systemic signs.  
4) Hyperphosphataemia  
   - Pros: Can cause bone changes.  
   - Cons: Rare in infants; periosteal reaction less typical; no other biochemical signs given.  
5) Ewing sarcoma  
   - Pros: Periosteal reaction common; cortical destruction.  
   - Cons: Typically shows aggressive bone destruction, soft tissue mass, diffusion restriction; no solid mass seen; mild STIR hyperintensity but no diffusion restriction; age less typical but possible.  
6) Scurvy  
   - Pros: Can cause cortical irregularity and periosteal reaction.  
   - Cons: Usually metaphyseal involvement; no dietary history; periosteal reaction usually more subtle; swelling is less typical.  
7) Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease)  
   - Pros: Classic age (infants), sudden onset swelling of soft tissues, periosteal reaction with cortical thickening and sclerosis especially over mandible; no abscess; MRI findings consistent; usually benign and self-limited.  
   - Cons: None significant; fits well.  
8) Malignant infantile hyperpetrosis  
   - Pros: Rare, can involve bone.  
   - Cons: No mass or aggressive findings; no soft tissue mass; no diffusion restriction; unlikely.  
9) Prolonged PGE1 infusion  
   - Pros: Can cause periostitis.  
   - Cons: No history of PGE1 infusion; rare and usually diffuse; no systemic context.

C) Ranking:  
1) Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease) - matches age, location (hemimandible), imaging with cortical thickening, sclerosis, periosteal reaction, no abscess or mass, mild MRI changes, clinical intermittent swelling.  
2) Osteomyelitis - possible but less likely due to lack of abscess, diffusion restriction, or systemic signs.  
3) Ewing sarcoma - less likely due to absence of aggressive bone destruction or soft tissue mass.  
4) Others - less likely with given findings.

Thus, the best fit is Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease).

</analysis>  
<final> Infantile cortical hyperostosis (Caffey disease) </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 2-year-old male child was brought by his 34-year-old mother with complaints of abdominal pain and recurrent vomiting. The child was clinically stable, and the abdominal ultrasound was normal. However, we incidentally discovered that both the mother and the son had physical impairments in their bilateral upper and lower limbs.
 Imaging Findings: The child had a classical median cleft with rudimentary bilateral thumbs with loss of opposition movement (Figure 1). A frontal radiograph revealed a bilateral median cleft, and the right hand’s proximal phalanges of the middle and index fingers fused, resulting in syndactyly. An additional middle phalanx was observed in the right thumb. Bilaterally, the thumb and index finger were displaced toward each other (Figures 2a and 2b). The radiography of the child’s feet depicted the absence of three of his metatarsals (Figure 2c). The X-ray of the infant’s skull showed multiple convolutions, suggesting a copper-beaten appearance (Figure 3).
 
 Interestingly, his 34-year-old mother, too, suffered from a similar deformity. On clinical inspection, both her right and left hands showed the presence of only three fingers (Figure 4a). A hypoplastic index finger was seen in the left hand. Bilateral feet showed only two toes with possible syndactyly (Figure 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 33-year-old female presents with a 6-year history of abnormal involuntary movements of all limbs, face, and lips, which initially started as increased fidgeting and finger flicking. Cognitive decline began a year later, with memory loss, disorientation, and difficulty dressing and eating, and now is fully dependent for activities of daily living.
 Imaging Findings: A contrast-enhanced MRI (CEMRI) of the brain was performed with 1.5T with sequences of T1WI inversion recovery 3D, T2WI axial and coronal, 3D FLAIR, SWI, DWI, and MR spectroscopy (MRS). MRI revealed atrophy of the head of the caudate bilaterally (Figures 1 and 2) with decreased FH:CC ratio of 1.18 (frontal horn width to intercaudate distance ratio) (Figure 5) and increased CC:IT ratio of 0.25 (intercaudate distance to inner table width ratio) (Figure 6). Volume loss of the putamen and globus pallidus was noted bilaterally. An enlargement of the bilateral frontal horns of the lateral ventricles, giving a box-like configuration, is noted. There is evidence of T2/FLAIR hyperintensities (Figures 3 and 4) seen in the involved region with mild grade II mineralisation seen in globi pallidi, extending to bilateral posterolateral putamen and also in the substantia nigra. Mild prominence of sulci and ventricles with grey matter volume loss is suggestive of mild cerebral volume loss. MRS shows choline peak and mild NAA (N-acetyl aspartate) loss in the basal ganglia (Figures 7a, 7b, 7c, and 7d).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: 8-year-old female with swelling in the dorsal aspect of the tongue on the left side, present since birth, with slight enlargement in the past 6 months. No history of fever, breathing difficulties, or bleeding tendency. On physical examination, it showed a bluish swelling in the anterior tongue measuring 1.5 x 1 cm, and it was a soft, non-tender mass on palpation (Figure 1).
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound of the dorsal aspect of the tongue showed a well-circumscribed hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No evidence of calcifications (phleboliths) or cystic areas. No surrounding induration was seen. Colour Doppler studies showed mild internal vascularity (Figure 2).
 
 MRI showed a well-defined T2 intensely hyperintense (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c), and iso to slightly hyperintense signal in T1-weighted images (Figure 5) in the anterior aspect of the tongue on the left side. No blooming artefacts on the gradient echo sequences (GRE) images (Figure 4). No evidence of true diffusion restriction. Homogeneous contrast enhancement on post-contrast T1 images was seen (Figure 6). Delayed post-contrast images are not available due to patient motion artefacts.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 6-year-old child with a diagnosis of Gorlin syndrome, so far manifested by medulloblastoma resected two years ago, and disease-free in subsequent follow-ups until now, presented with insidious hip pain. Given her medical history, a pelvic CT was performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI, performed 2 years previously, at the time of medulloblastoma diagnosis, revealed a mottled intraparenchymal mass in the posterior fossa with heterogeneous enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 1a and 1b).
 
 Actual pelvic CT showed extensive osteolytic involvement with a soft tissue component affecting all included bony structures (vertebral bodies, pelvic girdle, and femurs), with severe destruction of the pelvis and both femoral heads (Figures 2a and 2b). Given the presence of lytic metastases, a subsequent whole-body CT with intravenous contrast in the portal phase was performed, revealing retroperitoneal adenopathic conglomerates (Figure 3) and a single hepatic metastasis (Figure 4).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 56-year-old woman presented with a painless right breast lump, present for three months. She also reported easy fatigability, ptosis, and exertional dyspnoea for one year. She was diagnosed with myasthenia gravis, and was started on immunosuppressants five months ago. There was no history of tuberculosis (TB) or contact with TB patients.
 Imaging Findings: High-resolution CT of the thorax showed a well-defined soft tissue density in the anterior superior mediastinum. CT-guided biopsy revealed polygonal epithelial cells with interspersed lymphocytes, suggestive of thymoma type B1. Only non-contrast-enhanced CT of the thorax was performed (Figures 1a, 1b, and 1c) due to contraindication to iodinated contrast in myasthenia gravis. An elliptical pleural-based hyperdense lesion with peripheral calcification and central fluid density was noted along the lateral right hemithorax, suggestive of empyema. A lobulated soft tissue lesion was seen in the lower inner quadrant of the right breast, extending into the underlying pectoral muscle. Ultrasound correlation was advised.
 
 Ultrasound of the right breast (Figures 2a, 2b, and 3) revealed an irregular, heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with posterior enhancement, oriented parallel to the parenchyma from 3 to 5 o’clock position, 2 cm from the nipple–areola complex. No internal vascularity or calcification was seen. The lesion extended into the pectoralis muscle and appeared soft on elastography. It was categorised as BIRADS 4A.
 
 Fine-needle aspiration cytology was advised (Figures 4a and 4b).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 18-year-old female who recently migrated with her family due to war presented with longstanding, progressive motor impairment of unclear origin. She was referred by an outpatient neurologist after multiple sclerosis had been excluded. No previous medical records were available from her country of origin.
 
 According to her mother, she developed normally in her early infancy, walking independently. Around 17–18 months, shortly after vaccination (possibly MMR), she developed dragging of one leg, recurrent fevers, and loss of motor milestones. A verbal diagnosis of post-viral encephalitis was given at the time. Her condition fluctuated, but cognition remained intact. She developed progressive gait decline during her childhood. Wheelchair use began at age 15, and she is now fully dependent for transfers, though she eats and writes independently.
 
 Clinical and neurological examination revealed esophoria, mild tongue deviation, weak facial muscles, hypotrophic upper extremities with reduced fine motor control, joint hypermobility, increased tone and reflexes with bilateral positive Trömner signs, and severe leg-dominant spastic tetraparesis.
 
 Given the unclear clinical presentation, brain and spinal MRI were performed.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI revealed symmetrical T2 (Figures 1) and FLAIR (Figures 2) hyperintensities in the supratentorial white matter (excluding U-fibres), splenium of the corpus callosum, posterior limb of the internal capsule, pons along trigeminal pathways, inferior cerebellar peduncles, and pyramidal tracts of the medulla oblongata. DWI (Figures 3) showed restricted diffusion with low ADC (Figures 4). No contrast enhancement was observed (Figures 5). MR spectroscopy (Figures 6) revealed a lactate peak at the level of the abnormal white matter. Spinal MRI (Figures 7 and 8) displayed longitudinal signal abnormalities predominantly involving the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts throughout the entire cord. Findings suggested a metabolic leukodystrophy. Diagnosis was confirmed by genetic testing.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 21-year-old Caucasian man, an amateur drummer, presents with a two-year history of progressive weakness of the left distal hand. There is no associated pain or sensory loss. Past medical and family history are unremarkable. There is no history of preceding trauma.
 Imaging Findings: On MRI performed with the neck in the neutral position, T2 and T1-weighted sagittal images (Figures 1 and 2, respectively) reveal mild spinal cord atrophy at the C5–C7 level. On T2 axial images (Figure 3), atrophy of the left anterior horn is clearly visible.
 
 In the flexed position (Figure 4), there is an anterior shift of the posterior wall of the cervical dural sac at the C3–Th3 level. A crescent-shaped epidural venous plexus appears in the posterior epidural space, showing high signal intensity on both T1 and T2-weighted sequences and is accompanied by flow voids.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 81-year-old man was referred with a four-month history of persistent right upper quadrant pain radiating to the back. Liver function tests were normal, and he denied fever or pruritus. His medical history included pacemaker implantation, malaria, appendicectomy, arterial hypertension, and orthopaedic surgeries. No alcohol abuse or smoking was reported.
 Imaging Findings: The patient first underwent abdominal ultrasound and contrast-enhanced CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b), which revealed consistent findings: gallbladder hydrops with thin walls, moderate dilatation of the intrahepatic right bile ducts, and an ill-defined 1.5 cm soft-tissue lesion at the junction of the cystic duct and the common bile duct.
 
 Gadoxetic acid-enhanced MRI (Figures 2a and 2b) with magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 3a and 3b) confirmed tissue infiltration at the junction of the right anterior segmental duct and common bile duct, explaining the dilatation of the right anterior bile ducts.
 
 Endoscopic ultrasound and endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) (Figure 4) were subsequently performed to perform biopsies and to place a plastic biliary stent.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: Case 1
 
 A 28-year-old patient with known homozygous sickle cell disease (HbSS) presented with chronic fatigue, diffuse abdominal pain, and hip joint discomfort exacerbated by walking.
 
 Case 2
 
 A 34-year-old patient with a known history of sickle cell disease underwent CT for left upper quadrant abdominal pain and chronic fatigue.
 Imaging Findings: Case 1
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 1a and 1b) revealed a markedly reduced spleen size, with the parenchyma completely replaced by dense, coarse calcifications. The spleen had irregular but preserved contours. Post-contrast CT images did not show any enhancement within the splenic parenchyma. There was no perisplenic collection or lymphadenopathy.
 
 Bone window CT images through the pelvis revealed bilateral femoral head avascular necrosis (AVN), with subchondral collapse and cortical irregularities (Figures 2a and 2b).
 
 Case 2
 
 Abdominal CT scan (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, and 3d) demonstrated a severely atrophied spleen appearing as a densely calcified structure in the left upper quadrant. The calcifications are confluent and coarse, with no visible soft tissue component. Post-contrast images showed no contrast enhancement, and no other abdominal or bony abnormalities were identified.

Bone window CT images through the pelvis revealed bilateral femoral head avascular necrosis (AVN), with subchondral collapse and cortical irregularities. Case 2: Abdominal CT scan demonstrated a severely atrophied spleen appearing as a densely calcified structure in the left upper quadrant. The calcifications are confluent and coarse, with no visible soft tissue component. Post-contrast images showed no contrast enhancement, and no other abdominal or bony abnormalities were identified.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 23-year-old female presented with acute pain and swelling in her left ankle following a fall down the stairs. Clinical examination showed severe bilateral pes planus and mild hindfoot varus.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographic evaluation demonstrated flattening and contour irregularity of the middle and lateral thirds of the navicular bone, accompanied by sclerotic bone remodelling (Figure 1a). A step-off deformity was noted at the dorsal aspect between the navicular and cuneiform bones on the lateral view, along with flattening of the talar dome (Figure 1b).
 
 MRI confirmed these findings, revealing deformity and flattening of the central and lateral portions of the navicular bone, with bone marrow oedema predominantly in the central and dorsal regions. In addition, fragmentation of the central navicular bone was observed (Figures 2a, 2b, 3a, 3b, and 4).
PostDescription:

MRI confirmed these findings, revealing deformity and flattening of the central and lateral portions of the navicular bone, with bone marrow oedema predominantly in the central and dorsal regions. In addition, fragmentation of the central navicular bone was observed.
DifferentialDiagnosisList: Köhler disease (childhood navicular osteochondrosis), Müller–Weiss syndrome, Osteoarthritis, Navicular stress fracture, Charcot neuropathic osteoarthropathy

- Flattening of talar dome.

- Chronic remodeling features (flattening, sclerosis) coexist with acute injury findings (bone marrow edema, fragmentation).

B) Candidate pass/fail:
1) Köhler disease (childhood navicular osteochondrosis) -
   Pros: Age is off (Köhler disease occurs in children, typically 4-7 years old). Flattening and sclerosis of navicular can be seen. However, patient is an adult (23 years old), so less likely.
   Cons: Age mismatch, Köhler disease is self-limiting and usually unilateral; no mention of trauma; step-off deformity and talar dome flattening are not typical features.

2) MullerâWeiss syndrome -
   Pros: Adult patients with chronic navicular deformity and fragmentation; flattening of navicular bone, sclerosis, fragmentation are classic. Also, deformity of talar dome and foot alignment abnormalities (pes planus) can be associated. Trauma can precipitate symptoms.
   Cons: Usually presents in middle-aged adults with chronic midfoot pain; however, trauma can cause acute symptoms.

3) Osteoarthritis -
   Pros: Talar dome flattening could be arthritis. However, no mention of joint space narrowing, osteophytes.
   Cons: Age is young, osteoarthritis unlikely; navicular fragmentation and bone edema are not typical osteoarthritic changes.

4) Navicular stress fracture -
   Pros: Young adult with acute pain after trauma, bone marrow edema on MRI; possible fracture lines? However, no discrete fracture line described.
   Cons: Flattening and sclerosis of navicular are chronic changes, not typical of isolated stress fracture; step-off deformity and talar dome flattening do not fit.

5) Charcot neuropathic osteoarthropathy -
   Pros: Bone marrow edema, fragmentation, sclerosis, deformities.
   Cons: Usually in diabetic neuropathy or other neuropathies; no mention of neuropathy or diabetes. Patient is 23, less typical; acute trauma with bilateral pes planus but no neuropathy.

C) Ranking:
1) MullerâWeiss syndrome stands out due to deformity and fragmentation of navicular (especially central and lateral), sclerosis, talar dome flattening, and foot deformities (pes planus, varus) which are common in MullerâWeiss.
2) Navicular stress fracture possible but lacks classic features and chronic changes suggestive of stress fracture alone are insufficient.
3) Köhler disease is unlikely due to patient age and bilateral foot deformities not fitting classic presentation.
4) Charcot neuropathic osteoarthropathy unlikely without neuropathy context.
5) Osteoarthritis is the least likely given age and findings.

Thus, the best fit is MullerâWeiss syndrome.
</analysis>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 12-year-old boy without significant previous medical history presented in the ultrasound department for evaluation of a palpable lump noticed since the last two weeks in the left breast. The lump was non-tender and showed no changes in the overlying skin. There was no history of medication use. The hormonal profile and routine CBC profile were normal.
 Imaging Findings: Focused high-resolution ultrasound of both breasts and each axilla was performed using a high-frequency linear probe. The ultrasound identified a well-defined, well-encapsulated, hypoechoic lesion with smooth margins (Figure 1). It measured 3.8 x 2 x 3 cm in size and involved the left breast at the 12 o’clock periareolar region. The lesion was oriented parallel to the tissue planes (Figure 3). A single gentle lobulation was seen in this lesion. No significant vascularity was displayed on the colour Doppler study (Figure 2). The right breast was normal, and both axillae were normal.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 4-month-old male child from a tuberculosis (TB) endemic region presented with complaints of poor feeding, loss of weight for a month, and a low-grade, evening fever without any associated rash or chills for 15 days. The patient also had jerky movements of the right upper and lower limbs without any other signs of seizure disorder.
 
 The patient showed positive Kernig’s (pain and resistance to knee extension on flexing the patient’s hip and knee to 90 degrees and then attempting to extend the knee) [1] and Brudziński’s signs (in a patient with supine position, positive Brudziński’s is involuntary flexion of the hips and knees when the patient’s neck is passively flexed) [2].
 Imaging Findings: As the patient showed positive Kernig’s and Brudziński’s signs, meningeal TB was suspected. However, the chest radiograph was normal. On MRI, subtle T2 FLAIR (fluid attenuation inversion recovery) hyperintense minimally enhancing foci were seen in the bilateral cerebral cortex, caudate nucleus, thalami, and right hemipons with diffusion restriction and without blooming on gradient echo (GRE) sequences (Figures 1a and 1b). Subtle areas of leptomeningeal enhancement along the bilateral frontoparietal region were also seen. Mild communicating hydrocephalus was seen.
 
 A follow-up scan was advised after 4 months of antituberculosis treatment (ATT), as the patient had reduced activity, drowsiness, and episodes of seizure. On follow-up MRI, multiple conglomerated T2 hyperintense T1 fat-sat ring-enhancing lesions were seen in bilateral gangliocapsular and periventricular regions with leptomeningeal enhancement and hydrocephalus (Figures 2a and 2b). Mild surrounding cerebral oedema was also noted.
PostDescription:

DifferentialDiagnosisList: Cerebral abscess, Neurocysticercosis, Space-occupying lesion, Subacute infarct/haemorrhage, Disease progression/Drug-resistant tuberculosis, Paradoxical tuberculomas post-antituberculosis treatment
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- 4-month-old male infant from a TB endemic region with signs suggestive of meningitis (low-grade fever, positive Kernig’s and Brudzińskiâs signs).  
- Initially subtle T2 FLAIR hyperintense, minimally enhancing foci in bilateral cerebral cortex, caudate nucleus, thalami, and right hemipons with diffusion restriction, no GRE blooming.  
- Subtle leptomeningeal enhancement in bilateral frontoparietal region and mild communicating hydrocephalus.  
- After 4 months of anti-tuberculosis treatment (ATT), multiple conglomerated T2 hyperintense, T1 fat-sat ring-enhancing lesions in bilateral gangliocapsular and periventricular regions with leptomeningeal enhancement and mild surrounding edema. Mild hydrocephalus still present.  
- Clinical deterioration despite therapy (reduced activity, drowsiness, seizures).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Cerebral abscess  
   Pros: Ring-enhancing lesions, surrounding edema.  
   Cons: Bilateral symmetrical involvement and distribution in deep brain structures (gangliocapsular, periventricular) and caudate, thalami, hemipons is unusual; absence of GRE blooming (usually present in abscess due to hemorrhage or necrosis); presence of leptomeningeal enhancement and systemic TB signs argue against simple abscess.  

2) Neurocysticercosis  
   Pros: Ring-enhancing lesions can mimic these findings.  
   Cons: Patient age (4 months) is too young for clinical neurocysticercosis; typical lesions in basal ganglia are rare; concomitant meningitis and leptomeningeal enhancement uncommon; no history of exposure; no typical "cyst with dot" sign; patient from TB endemic area with meningitis signs points away from NCC.  

3) Space-occupying lesion  
   Pros: Ring-enhancing lesions suggest neoplasm.  
   Cons: Multiple lesions in deep gray matter and periventricular areas uncommon for primary neoplasm in infants; diffuse leptomeningeal enhancement and clinical context of meningitis are not typical; unlikely multiple primary tumors in symmetric distribution; clinical picture of infection vs tumor.  

4) Subacute infarct/haemorrhage  
   Pros: Diffusion restriction and T2 FLAIR hyperintensity can be seen in infarcts.  
   Cons: Multiple bilateral deep gray and brainstem lesions in infant unlikely infarcts; no vascular risk factors; presence of leptomeningeal enhancement and hydrocephalus not explained; ring-enhancing lesions on follow-up inconsistent without infarction.  

5) Disease progression/Drug-resistant tuberculosis  
   Pros: Clinical deterioration despite ATT; presence of leptomeningeal enhancement and ring-enhancing lesions suggesting ongoing infection/inflammation; typical in multidrug-resistant or inadequately treated TB meningitis.  
   Cons: Treatment had been ongoing for 4 months; no direct evidence of drug resistance on imaging, but clinical failure supports disease progression despite therapy.  

6) Paradoxical tuberculomas post-antituberculosis treatment  
   Pros: Paradoxical reaction with new or worsening tuberculomas after initiation of ATT is known, especially in TB meningitis; ring-enhancing lesions, leptomeningeal enhancement, and clinical worsening despite treatment are classic findings; MR imaging findings typical of tuberculomas (T2 hyperintensity, ring enhancement, mild edema) in deep brain structures and periventricular areas.  
   Cons: Requires careful clinical correlation, but fits well given the context and MR evolution.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Paradoxical tuberculomas post-antituberculosis treatment (matches clinical deterioration and new ring-enhancing lesions after ATT initiation, pathognomonic in TB meningitis paradoxical response).  
2) Disease progression/Drug-resistant tuberculosis (plausible but less specific than paradoxical reaction).  
3) Cerebral abscess (less likely due to lesion distribution and absence of GRE blooming).  
4) Subacute infarct/haemorrhage (excluded by distribution and clinical context).  
5) Neurocysticercosis (excluded by age and clinical context).  
6) Space-occupying lesion (least likely given clinical and imaging findings).  

<final>Paradoxical tuberculomas post-antituberculosis treatment</final><|return|>
options: Cerebral abscess | Neurocysticercosis | Space-occupying lesion | Subacute infarct/haemorrhage | Disease progression/Drug-resistant tuberculosis | Paradoxical tuberculomas post-antituberculosis treatment

Clinical History: An eight-month-old female child presented with jaundice and abdominal distension. Clinical examination revealed icterus, a distended stomach, and prominent superficial veins. Laboratory reports showed elevated serum glutamic pyruvic transaminase and serum glutamic oxaloacetic transaminase levels with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound (USG) showed grossly coarsened echotexture of liver with multiple tiny communicating and non-communicating cystic lesions (Figures 1a and 1c). A slightly irregular liver outline was also noted (Figure 1c), indicating liver cirrhosis. Figure 1b shows a large intrahepatic cyst near the porta hepatis containing echogenic debris in dependent location, measuring 2.2 x 1.1 cm (indicating Caroli disease). Many sections also showed ill-defined hypoechoic nodules in the liver, indicative of dysplastic nodules (Figure 1d). The gallbladder was distended, showing an elongated gallbladder neck with a thickened wall (2.4 mm) and increased pericholecystic echogenicity (Figure 2a). Mild dilatation of the cystic duct was also noted (Figure 2b). Another section showed both liver and right kidney in profile with increased renal parenchymal echotexture (Figure 3). Colour Doppler assessment showed absent colour flow in cystic structures, suggesting cystic lesions to be biliary dilatations (Figures 4a and 4b). A splenomegaly of 8 cm with a dilated (10 mm) splenic vein was noted (Figure 6).

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: An 11-year-old girl with normal hearing and cognition had severe dysarthria and language delay since birth. Neurological exam showed no motor deficits but significant expressive impairment. Given the persistent speech delay, a brain MRI was requested for further evaluation.
 Imaging Findings: Brain MRI images demonstrated the eye-of-the-tiger sign. There is marked hypointensity of the globus pallidus on T2-weighted and FLAIR sequences secondary to iron deposition, containing minimal dot-like hyperintensities (vacuolisation), associated with comma-shaped high-signal intensity in the antero-medial part of the globus pallidus, likely due to gliosis (Figures 1 and 2). There is also marked hypointensity on both T2*EG and DWI (diffusion-weighted images) sequences due to susceptibility effects (Figures 3 and 4).
PostDescription:

<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Age: 11-year-old girl with severe dysarthria and language delay since birth.  
- No motor deficits but significant expressive language impairment.  
- Brain MRI shows classic "eye-of-the-tiger" sign: marked T2/FLAIR hypointensity of the globus pallidus due to iron deposition with central dot-like hyperintensities (vacuolisation) and cherry/ comma-shaped high T2 signal in the anteromedial globus pallidus likely representing gliosis.  
- Marked hypointensity on T2* and DWI due to susceptibility effects (iron).  
- These imaging findings are characteristic and pathognomonic for a specific subgroup within NBIA disorders, especially pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN).  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Wilson's disease:  
   Pros: Can have basal ganglia abnormalities and liver involvement on imaging.  
   Cons: Typically shows more varied and diffuse basal ganglia changes without the pathognomonic eye-of-the-tiger sign; clinical background often includes hepatic dysfunction and different age range. Iron deposition pattern different; no eye-of-the-tiger sign.  
   
2) Aceruloplasminemia:  
   Pros: Iron deposition in basal ganglia and brain.  
   Cons: Usually presents in adults; MRI does not typically show eye-of-the-tiger sign; clinical presentation different (anemia, diabetes, retinal degeneration).  
   
3) Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) disorder:  
   Pros: NBIA is an umbrella term that includes various disorders with brain iron deposition; eye-of-the-tiger sign falls within NBIA spectrum.  
   Cons: Too broad; we have a specific imaging pattern that aligns with a specific NBIA subtype.  
   
4) Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN):  
   Pros: Classic eye-of-the-tiger sign in globus pallidus is pathognomonic; age and clinical presentation (speech delay, dysarthria) fit classic/early onset PKAN.  
   Cons: None apparent.  
   
5) Infantile bilateral striatal necrosis:  
   Pros: Can affect basal ganglia.  
   Cons: Usually unilateral or asymmetric; imaging findings different; no eye-of-the-tiger sign.  
   
6) Organophosphate poisoning:  
   Pros: Can affect basal ganglia; acute toxic exposure.  
   Cons: Clinical history does not fit acute poisoning; imaging pattern not consistent.  

C) Ranking and tie-break:  
1) Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration (PKAN) - best fits clinical and imaging (eye-of-the-tiger sign is pathognomonic).  
2) Neurodegeneration with brain iron accumulation (NBIA) disorder - umbrella term includes PKAN, but less specific.  
3) Wilson's disease - imaging and clinical mismatch.  
4) Aceruloplasminemia, Infantile bilateral striatal necrosis, Organophosphate poisoning - less likely given clinical and imaging findings.  
Thus, the best diagnosis is Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration.  
</analysis>  
<final>  
Pantothenate kinase-associated neurodegeneration  
</final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A nine-year-old boy, previously healthy, presented with fever, cough, dyspnoea, vomiting and diarrhoea. Eighteen days after hospital admission, he had the first spontaneous bilateral pneumothorax, needing drainage, intensive care unit admission, and mechanical ventilation. After 3 months, even with remaining lung lesions and pneumothorax, he could be discharged for outpatient follow-up.
 Imaging Findings: The initial imaging exam was a chest radiograph (X-ray) showing a bilateral diffuse micronodular acinar infiltrate (Figures 1a and 1b). A computed tomography (CT) was performed, which revealed a diffuse micronodular pattern and random distribution throughout the lung parenchyma, suggestive of an inflammatory/infectious process (Figures 2a, 2b, and 2c).
 
 The patient experienced several pneumothoraces, all of which were drained. Up to three chest tubes were placed simultaneously (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c, 3d, 3e, and 3f). A second contrast-enhanced chest CT revealed confluent cystic lesions, prevailing on the upper lobes and posterior regions (Figures 4a, 4b, 4c, 4d, 4e, and 4f).
 
 A few weeks later, another contrast-enhanced chest CT showed the same findings, but with larger pneumothoraces on both sides, despite the presence of three thoracic drains (Figures 5a, 5b, 5c, 5d, and 5e). Pneumomediastinum was noted.
 
 Before hospital discharge, the patient underwent a new contrast-enhanced chest CT. The lung abnormalities persisted, but the patient was able to tolerate them even after the chest tubes were removed (Figures 6a, 6b, and 6c).
 
 Eight months later, a follow-up chest CT revealed almost complete recovery of the pulmonary abnormalities, with sequelae changes and discreet cystic lesions, and with favourable evolution of the imaging findings over the eleven-month follow-up period (Figures 7a, 7b, and 7c).

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient: 9-year-old boy, previously healthy.
- Clinical presentation: fever, cough, dyspnea, vomiting, diarrhea.
- Timeline: 18 days after admission, first spontaneously bilateral pneumothorax; required drainage, ICU admission, mechanical ventilation.
- Imaging: Initial CXR showed bilateral diffuse micronodular acinar infiltrate.
- CT showed diffuse micronodular pattern with random distribution; suggests inflammatory/infectious process.
- Multiple pneumothoraces, requiring up to 3 chest tubes.
- Follow-up CTs showed progression to confluent cystic lesions, especially in upper lobes and posterior regions, worsening pneumothoraces, pneumomediastinum.
- Follow-up over months shows gradual improvement and near-complete resolution, with some residual cystic lesions.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1) Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia:
- Pros: Micronodular infiltrates, cyst formation, pneumothorax known complication.
- Cons: Typically in immunocompromised hosts or neonates; patient was previously healthy; the timeline and severe pneumothorax is unusual but documented; cystic lesions are usually diffuse and can be more severe; improvement over months is typical with treatment, but multiple severe bilateral pneumothoraces are rare.

2) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia (LIP):
- Pros: Micronodular infiltrates, cyst formation; young patients can be affected.
- Cons: Usually seen in patients with connective tissue disease or immunodeficiency; no mention of systemic disease here; pneumothorax less common; cysts tend to be more diffuse and bilateral.

3) Langerhans cell histiocytosis:
- Pros: Cystic lung disease with upper lobe predominance; pneumothorax is a known complication.
- Cons: Usually adolescent smokers; rare in children younger than 10; smoking history denied; no prior risk factors; usually nodules with cavitation progressing to cysts, not initial micronodular pattern exclusively; also systemic symptoms sometimes present.

4) Cystic pulmonary tuberculosis:
- Pros: Can cause cystic lesions and pneumothorax; systemic symptoms present.
- Cons: TB cystic lesions less common in children; nodular pattern less typical; multifocal pneumothorax rare; TB usually shows cavitary lesions and lymphadenopathy; cystic changes rarely as extensive and widespread; time course and recovery also different.

5) Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM):
- Pros: Cystic lung disease with pneumothorax.
- Cons: Almost exclusively affects women of childbearing age; extremely rare in children/males; clinical history does not fit.

6) Birt-Hogg-Dubé syndrome:
- Pros: Multiple cysts, spontaneous pneumothorax.
- Cons: Genetic syndrome usually presents in adults; very rare in children; other systemic findings like skin/renal cysts absent; no family history mentioned.

C) Ranking:
1) Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia: best fits the clinical presentation with acute infectious process, micronodular infiltrates, cystic changes, pneumothoraces, favorable response to treatment.
2) Langerhans cell histiocytosis: cystic lung disease with pneumothorax, but age and lack of smoking history are against.
3) Cystic pulmonary tuberculosis: possible but less likely due to rarity of extensive cystic disease and bilateral pneumothoraces in children.
4) Lymphoid interstitial pneumonia: less likely without underlying disease.
5) Birt-Hogg-Dubé: unlikely in age and clinical context.
6) Lymphangioleiomyomatosis: excluded by sex and age.

Final choice: Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia.
</analysis>

<final> Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 73-year-old female patient underwent follow-up at our institution for 14 years due to a pancreatic head lesion. The lesion was monitored at six-month intervals through ultrasound, computed tomography, or magnetic resonance imaging, revealing sustained growth throughout the surveillance period. However, the patient remained asymptomatic, and laboratory tests were unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: Cross-sectional imaging of the upper abdomen, namely computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), revealed a multicystic lesion in the pancreatic head, with lobulated margins, enhancing internal septations (Figures 1, 2, and 3) and a central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications (Figures 1a–1h). On ultrasound, the lesion depicts multiple hypoechoic/anechoic cysts and hyperechoic septations (Figure 4).
 
 Over time, the lesion’s enlargement led to gradual dilatation of both the bile ducts and the main pancreatic duct. In the most recent magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) (Figures 2a–2i), the pancreatic lesion measured approximately 97 x 86 x 126 mm (length x width x anteroposterior axis). The common bile duct had a calibre of 15 mm, and the main pancreatic duct had a maximum calibre of 7 mm. The pancreatic parenchyma was atrophic.
PostDescription:

DifferentialDiagnosisList: Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas, Macrocystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas, Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas, Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN) of the pancreas, Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas
FinalDiagnosis: Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas
model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>
A) Key findings:
- Patient: 73-year-old female, asymptomatic, normal labs.
- Lesion location: Pancreatic head.
- Imaging over 14 years: Multicystic lesion with lobulated margins.
- Enhancing internal septations.
- Central fibrotic scar with stellate calcifications.
- Ultrasound: Multiple hypoechoic/anechoic cysts and hyperechoic septations.
- Over time: Lesion enlarged, causing gradual dilatation of both common bile duct (CJ) and main pancreatic duct (MPD).
- Size: 97 x 86 x 126 mm.
- Common bile duct calibre: 15 mm (dilated).
- MPD calibre: 7 mm (dilated).
- Parenchymal atrophy of pancreas.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas:
   Pros:
   - Classic imaging features include a multicystic lesion with many small cysts.
   - Characteristic central stellate scar with calcifications is pathognomonic.
   - Lobulated margins and internal septations enhancing are typical.
   - Chronic slow growth over years without symptoms fits benign nature.
   - "Giant" due to large size (~10 cm) is consistent.
   Cons:
   - No major contradictions.

2) Macrocystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas:
   Pros:
   - Also a serous cystadenoma variant, can have fewer but larger cysts.
   Cons:
   - Typically does not have central scar with stellate calcifications; usually more macroscopic cysts lacking stellate scar.
   - Central scar and stellate calcifications are microcystic hallmarks.

3) Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas:
   Pros:
   - Usually found in pancreatic body/tail (not head), in middle-aged women.
   Cons:
   - Lack of elevated labs; typically does not have central stellate scar or calcifications.
   - Cystic lesion is usually unilocular or few macrocysts, not many multicysts.
   - No mention of ovarian-type stroma or mucin.

4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm (IPMN):
   Pros:
   - Can cause dilatation of MPD and bile duct (if head involvement).
   Cons:
   - Typically involves the ductal system with communication; lesion described as multicystic with septations, not typical for IPMN.
   - No mucin-producing signs or ductal communication described.
   - Central scar and stellate calcifications are unusual.

5) Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm of the pancreas:
   Pros:
   - Usually young women; less likely at 73 years.
   Cons:
   - Usually solid-cystic with a heterogeneous solid component; here lesion is purely cystic with many septations.
   - No mention of solid components or hemorrhagic cysts.
   - Central scar and stellate calcifications not typical.

C) Ranking:
1) Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas – Pathognomonic central stellate scar with calcifications, multicystic lesion with septations, slow growth, benign nature.
2) Macrocystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas – Similar but less likely due to central scar and calcifications characteristic of microcystic type.
3) Mucinous cystic neoplasm – Less likely due to location, imaging features, and lack of mucin signs.
4) Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm – Dilatation of MPD and bile duct suggest IPMN, but lesion morphology and central scar argue against.
5) Solid pseudopapillary neoplasm – Unlikely given age, lesion character, and imaging.

</analysis><final> Giant microcystic serous cystadenoma of the pancreas </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 78-year-old female presented to the surgery outpatient department with complaints of painless swelling, posterior to the left knee, for 20 years, with a recent onset of pain (since 3 months) after getting hit by an animal at the site of swelling. On examination, the swelling was firm and lobulated with no overlying skin changes. There was a history of weight loss and loss of appetite. No other comorbidities. No family history of malignancy.
 Imaging Findings: A plain lateral radiograph of the knee demonstrated a soft tissue lesion seen on the posterior aspect of the knee (Figure 1).
 
 Soft tissue ultrasound (US) demonstrated a large lobulated and irregularly shaped heterogeneously hypoechoic mass lesion with internal vascularity, seen posterior to the left knee (Figure 2 ). A few central hypoechoic necrotic/cystic areas were also seen. The mass was also seen displacing the popliteal vessels superficially.
 
 MRI (with contrast) findings revealed a large T1 heterointense lobulated mass lesion in the popliteal fossa with patchy areas of T1 hyperintensity (Figure 3). It also showed heterointense signal on T2W images (Figure 4). The lesion also showed patchy areas of diffusion restriction on DWI and ADC (Figures 5a and 5b) and heterogeneous enhancement on post-contrast study (Figure 6). It was seen displacing and causing near-complete encasement of the popliteal neurovascular bundle. There was no obvious involvement/infiltration of the overlying skin.

A) Key findings:
- Elderly female (78 years) with a long-standing (20 years) painless, lobulated, firm swelling in the posterior knee (popliteal fossa), with recent pain after trauma.
- Imaging: Plain radiograph shows a soft tissue mass behind the knee.
- Ultrasound: Large lobulated, irregular hypoechoic mass with internal vascularity and central necrotic/cystic areas; displacing popliteal vessels superficially.
- MRI: Large lobulated T1 heterointense mass with patchy T1 hyperintensity, heterointense T2 signal, patchy diffusion restriction, heterogeneous contrast enhancement, encasing the popliteal neurovascular bundle without skin infiltration.

B) Candidate pass/fail with pros/cons:

1) Clear cell sarcoma:
   - Pros: Typically arises near tendons in extremities; T1/T2 heterogeneity with enhancement; can mimic melanoma histologically.
   - Cons: Usually occurs in younger patients; commonly in the foot or ankle region; popliteal fossa less common; no mention of melanocytic markers.

2) Malignant melanoma:
   - Pros: Could explain T1 hyperintensity due to melanin; can involve soft tissue; pigmented lesion.
   - Cons: Primary melanomas of deep soft tissues (non-cutaneous melanoma) are rare; no skin changes or melanoma history; lesion is deep and large with no infiltration of overlying skin.

3) Clear cell soft tissue sarcoma with melanocytic differentiation:
   - Pros: Known for melanin production, explaining T1 hyperintensity; can present as lobulated soft tissue mass; often in extremities; internal vascularity and necrosis common.
   - Cons: Rare tumor; but fits well with T1 hyperintensity due to melanin and location.

4) Pigment villonodular synovitis with malignant transformation:
   - Pros: Occurs in joints, can cause mass behind knee.
   - Cons: Typically causes low T1/T2 due to hemosiderin (blooming), not T1 hyperintensity; malignant transformation is rare; usually synovial origin rather than large soft tissue mass encasing vessels.

5) Synovial sarcoma:
   - Pros: Common in young adults but can occur older; often lobulated and heterogeneous with T1/T2 variable signals; internal vascularity common.
   - Cons: Typically anterior knee; no mention of calcifications; T1 hyperintensity is uncommon unless hemorrhage.

6) Alveolar soft part sarcoma:
   - Pros: T1 hyperintensity possible; occurs in extremities in young adults.
   - Cons: Usually in adolescents/young adults; less likely in this age group; no typical vascular encasement pattern.

7) Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour (MPNST):
   - Pros: Can occur in retrovascular spaces; can enlarge and encase vessels; heterogeneous mass.
   - Cons: Usually no T1 hyperintensity unless melanin present; no neurofibromatosis history or nerve involvement directly; rare melanotic differentiation.

C) Ranking:
1) Clear cell soft tissue sarcoma with melanocytic differentiation: Explained T1 hyperintensity with melanin; lobulated mass with necrosis and vascularity; encases neurovascular bundle; fits anatomy and imaging well.
2) Malignant melanoma: Primary lesion is rare in deep soft tissue without skin involvement; but melanin explains T1 hyperintensity.
3) Clear cell sarcoma: Similar but age and location less typical.
4) Synovial sarcoma and other sarcomas: Less likely due to T1 hyperintensity and location.
5) Pigment villonodular synovitis: Contradicted by T1 hyperintensity pattern.
6) Malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumour: No nerve sheath signs, melanin less likely.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 36-year-old male, HIV-1 positive since age 22 with no follow-up for 4 years, presented with progressive dyspnoea, cough, thoracic pain, and fatigue. Initially treated for presumed community-acquired pneumonia with partial symptom improvement, but later experienced worsening respiratory symptoms. Denied fever or night sweats.
 Imaging Findings: Chest X-ray revealed mediastinal widening, likely due to a mass, accompanied by ill-defined lower lung opacities and bilateral pleural effusion, more pronounced on the right (Figure 1).
 
 Thoracic CT demonstrated a large anterior mediastinal mass (10.8 × 5.3 × 7.6 cm), heterogeneously enhancing with cystic areas and displacement of adjacent vascular structures without invasion, likely representing a conglomerate of necrotic hypervascular lymph nodes (Figure 2). Additional mediastinal adenopathies were noted.
 
 Pulmonary findings included bilateral “flame-shaped” peribronchovascular opacities (Figure 3a), patchy ground-glass opacities, and interlobular septal thickening (Figure 3b). Bilateral pleural effusion was also evident. Systemic hypervascular adenopathies involved the axillary, mesenteric, and inguinal regions bilaterally (short diameter up to 1.7 cm) (Figures 4a, 4b, and 4c).
 
 Abdominal imaging revealed cutaneous millimetric nodules in the thoracic and abdominal walls (Figure 5), hepatomegaly (17.9 cm in craniocaudal length), and gastric wall thickening (Figures 6a and 6b).

A) Key findings:
- 36-year-old HIV-1 positive male with poor follow-up for 4 years.
- Clinical presentation: progressive dyspneas, cough, thoracic pain, fatigue, no fever or night sweats.
- Chest X-ray: mediastinal widening (mass), ill-defined lower lung opacities, bilateral pleural effusion (right > left).
- CT thorax: large anterior mediastinal mass (10.8 x 5.3 x 7.6 cm), heterogeneously enhancing with cystic areas, hypervascular nodes with probable necrosis, displacing but not invading adjacent vessels.
- Additional mediastinal adenopathy.
- Pulmonary findings: bilateral flame-shaped peribronchovascular opacities, patchy ground glass opacities, interlobular septal thickening.
- Bilateral systemic hypervascular lymphadenopathy (axillary, mesenteric, inguinal, up to 1.7 cm).
- Abdominal findings: multiple cutaneous micronodules in thoracic and abdominal walls, hepatomegaly (17.9 cm), gastric wall thickening.
- The lesion is hypervascular with cystic/necrotic areas.
- HIV positive, likely immunosuppressed.

B) Candidate pass/fail:

1) Lymphoma:
- Pros: Mediastinal mass with lymphadenopathy; common in HIV patients; can be hypervascular.
- Cons: Usually lacks such extensive skin, gastric wall, and cutaneous nodules; peribronchovascular flame-shaped opacities less typical; the hypervascular nature and cystic necrosis can be seen, but systemic hypervascularity plus gastric and skin involvement less common.

2) Atypical pneumonia:
- Pros: Respiratory symptoms; peribronchovascular opacities could be pneumonia.
- Cons: Mediastinal mass with necrotic lymph nodes less typical; hypervascular systemic lymphadenopathy and skin involvement not typical; pleural effusion bilateral but mass is too large for pneumonia alone.

3) Mycobacterial infection:
- Pros: HIV positive, immunosuppressed; mediastinal lymphadenopathy with necrosis; fever/ night sweats absent here though.
- Cons: Mycobacterial lymph nodes often hypodense with caseation but less hypervascular; peribronchovascular opacities possible but less likely to have hypervascular systemic adenopathy or cutaneous nodules; gastric and skin nodules are uncommon.

4) Multicentric Castleman disease:
- Pros: Large hypervascular anterior mediastinal mass, systemic hypervascular lymphadenopathy, pulmonary involvement with peribronchovascular opacities (common in pulmonary MCD), HIV positive common association, presence of systemic symptoms (fatigue).
- Cons: Less known for cutaneous nodules or gastric wall thickening, but skin involvement can occur; pleural effusion and hepatic involvement consistent; hypervascular lymphadenopathy hallmark.

5) Disseminated AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma:
- Pros: HIV positive; hypervascular lymphadenopathy; cutaneous nodules; gastric wall thickening typical site; hepatic involvement; hypervascular mediastinal nodes/mass possible; flame-shaped peribronchovascular opacities and pleural effusion can be seen.
- Cons: Mediastinal mass less commonly described as a bulky mass; lymph node necrosis less typical; but KS can involve lymph nodes and cause bulky disease rarely.

6) Lymphangitic carcinomatosis:
- Pros: Flame-shaped peribronchovascular opacities common; nodular thickening of interlobular septa.
- Cons: HIV patient less likely cancer; no primary tumor described; mediastinal mass less typical; cutaneous, gastric, systemic lymphadenopathy less typical.

7) Pulmonary oedema:
- Pros: Interlobular septal thickening present.
- Cons: Does not explain anterior mediastinal mass, hypervascular lymphadenopathy, skin nodules, gastric thickening; clinical picture different.

8) Sarcoidosis:
- Pros: Mediastinal lymphadenopathy, bilateral lung involvement.
- Cons: Hypervascularity, cystic necrotic mediastinal mass less typical; skin nodules possible but rarely gastric wall involvement; HIV immunosuppressed patients less likely; pleural effusion minimal; not hypervascular.

C) Ranking:
1) Multicentric Castleman disease - fits best systemic hypervascular adenopathy, large hypervascular mediastinal mass, pulmonary findings, HIV positive, systemic involvement.
2) Disseminated AIDS-related Kaposi sarcoma - fits hypervascular lymphadenopathy, skin and gastric involvement in HIV patient; mediastinal mass less typical but possible.
3) Lymphoma - common but less explaining cutaneous and gastric findings and systemic hypervascularity.

Given hypervascular systemic adenopathy, mediastinal mass with cystic necrosis, pulmonary peribronchovascular flame-shaped opacities, gastric wall thickening, skin nodules, and HIV positivity, the most likely diagnosis is Multicentric Castleman disease.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 19-day-old female newborn was brought to our hospital because of left breast tumefaction for 2 days, with localised erythema and irritability. No fluctuance or nipple discharge was noted. No fever was present. The white blood cell count was elevated. The delivery was eutocic (40 weeks and 2 days) and unremarkable.
 Imaging Findings: The right breast was unremarkable (Figure 1a). The breast bud is hypoechoic relative to subcutaneous fat. The left lower quadrant’s mammary tissue was poorly marginated and more echogenic than the contralateral side (Figure 1b), with increased Doppler colour flow in the lower quadrants (Figure 2a). In the inferior aspect of the quadrants, an avascular area without colour flow was noted, measuring 4 x 3 x 3 mm, with through-transmission and surrounding hyperaemia (Figure 2b). The subcutaneous tissue was thick and hyperechoic.

model_answer_raw:
<|channel|>final<|message|><analysis>  
A) Key findings:  
- Patient: 19-day-old female newborn  
- Clinical presentation: Left breast tumefaction for 2 days, localized erythema, irritability, elevated white blood count, no fever  
- Imaging (ultrasound): Right breast normal; left breast: hypoechoic tissue relative to subcutaneous fat; poorly marginated increasingly echogenic mammary tissue in left lower quadrant; increased Doppler color flow in lower quadrants; an avascular area (no colour flow) measuring 4 x 3 x 3 mm with through-transmission and surrounding hyperaemia; thick, hyperechoic subcutaneous tissue  

B) Candidate pass/fail:  
1) Neonatal physiological breast hypertrophy  
   Pros: Common in newborn females; typically bilateral and symmetric enlargement of the breast buds; usually painless, with no erythema or systemic signs.  
   Cons: In this case, unilateral involvement with erythema, irritability, and leukocytosis is noted, which are not typical for physiological hypertrophy. Increased Doppler flow and avascular area with hyperaemia are atypical.  

2) Neonatal mastitis  
   Pros: Usually unilateral; features include erythema, swelling, irritability, leukocytosis; ultrasound shows hypoechoic tissue with increased vascularity (hyperemia) due to inflammation; subcutaneous tissue thickening and hyperechogenicity are consistent.  
   Cons: No fever present and no fluctuance, but these do not exclude mastitis.  

3) Neonatal mastitis with abscess  
   Pros: Same as mastitis plus abscess formation; ultrasound shows avascular area with through-transmission and surrounding hyperaemia; the avascular area measuring 4 x 3 x 3 mm could represent an early abscess or localized pus collection. Increased vascularity surrounding the avascular area supports abscess formation.  

4) Neonatal mastitis with cyst  
   Pros: A cystic area would appear avascular on Doppler.  
   Cons: Cysts generally do not have surrounding hyperaemia; cyst fluid usually anechoic rather than hyperechoic; clinical context less likely cyst.  

C) Ranking:  
1) Neonatal mastitis with abscess - Strongly supported by focal avascular area with surrounding hyperaemia, increased Doppler flow indicating inflammation plus abscess formation.  
2) Neonatal mastitis - Slightly less favored due to abscess-like imaging features.  
3) Neonatal physiological breast hypertrophy - Unlikely due to clinical and imaging inflammatory signs.  
4) Neonatal mastitis with cyst - Least likely given imaging features and clinical signs.

<final> Neonatal mastitis with abscess </final><|return|>

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 30‐year‐old man presented with two months of right knee pain and swelling. He had recovered from COVID‑19 pneumonia three months earlier after receiving systemic steroids. During his stay in the hospital, the patient has received dexamethasone injection 8 mg/day through the intravenous route for 10 days with gradual tapering over the next 2 weeks. No evidence of diabetes mellitus or other systemic comorbidities. Localised tenderness over the patella was noted; there were no systemic symptoms or history of trauma.
 Imaging Findings: Radiographs of the knee in anteroposterior, lateral, and skyline views revealed superficial sclerosis of the patella, along with a subtle erosion at its inferior pole (Figures 1a and 1b). There were no signs of fracture or significant soft tissue injury. Given the clinical context and radiographic findings, infection was suspected, and an MRI was recommended.
 
 On MRI, the patellar inferior pole appeared hypointense on T1 images (Figures 2a and 2b) and hyperintense on proton density (PD) fat-saturated sequences (Figures 3a, 3b, and 3c). Diffusion-weighted images (DWI) also indicated restriction (Figures 4a and 4b). The subchondral bone and articular cartilage remained largely intact, although a mild effusion was present. Post-contrast images showed thickening and enhancement of the synovium (Figures 5a and 5b). Osteomyelitis of the patella was considered likely.

A) Key findings:  
- Age: 30-year-old man with two months of right knee pain and swelling

- Physical exam: Localized tenderness over patella, no trauma history, no systemic symptoms

- MRI: Inferior pole of patella hypointense on T1, hyperintense on PD fat-sat and restricted on DWI, thickened and enhancing synovium, mild effusion, subchondral bone and cartilage largely intact  
- Impression: Osteomyelitis of the patella suspected  

B) Candidate pass/fail  
1) Post-traumatic bone contusion/fracture  
   - Pros: Patellar changes on imaging could be related to trauma  
   - Cons: No history of trauma; radiographs show sclerosis and erosion rather than acute fracture or bone bruise pattern; MRI shows infection signs (diffusion restriction, synovial enhancement) not typical for contusion/fracture  
2) Post-traumatic bone osteochondral defect  
   - Pros: Could explain focal patellar changes  
   - Cons: No trauma history; MRI does not show cartilage defect or osteochondral lesion; infection signs present  
3) Tendon/ligament injuries at the patellar insertion site  
   - Pros: Patellar insertion site involved; localized swelling  
   - Cons: Imaging shows bone marrow edema and infection signs, not typical tendon/ligament injury; MRI findings show bone involvement and synovial enhancement, not soft tissue/ligament injury  
4) Fungal osteomyelitis of the patella  
   - Pros: Clinical context of immunosuppression (steroid use); subtle bone erosion and marrow changes with diffusion restriction; synovial thickening and enhancement compatible with infection; no trauma history  
   - Cons: No direct evidence of fungal organism on imaging; however, radiologically indistinguishable from bacterial osteomyelitis  
5) Inflammatory arthritis  
   - Pros: Knee pain and swelling; synovial enhancement  
   - Cons: Focal patellar erosion and marrow edema uncommon; osteomyelitis pattern with diffusion restriction and bone erosion less typical; no systemic inflammatory signs reported  
6) Neoplasm (osteochondroma)  
   - Pros: Patellar lesion possible  
   - Cons: Osteochondroma is typically cortical bone protrusion with cartilage cap; no marrow edema or infection signs; MRI findings not consistent with neoplasm  
7) Neoplasm (chondroblastoma)  
   - Pros: Lesion in patella possible; MRI signal characteristics could overlap  
   - Cons: Usually epiphyseal lesion with cartilage matrix; no signs of infection or synovial enhancement; patient age compatible but clinical picture does not fit neoplasm  

C) Ranking  
1) Fungal osteomyelitis of the patella (match to infectious pattern, immunosuppressed context)  
2) Osteomyelitis (if bacterial not specified) - same as fungal but no organism spec  
3) Post-traumatic bone contusion/fracture (ruled out by no trauma)  
4) Post-traumatic osteochondral defect (also improbable)  
5) Inflammatory arthritis (less likely given focal bone erosion and infection signs)  
6) Neoplasm (chondroblastoma) (least consistent)  
7) Neoplasm (osteochondroma) (least consistent)  

Thus, the imaging findings of marrow edema, cortical erosion, diffusion restriction, synovial thickening/enhancement in an immunosuppressed patient with no trauma best support osteomyelitis, and given steroid therapy and post-COVID context, fungal infection is plausible.

7) Neoplasm (osteochondroma) (least consistent)

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 45-year-old female presented with a longstanding history of primary amenorrhea and cyclical lower abdominal pain since adolescence. The pain occurs every month, typically lasting 3–5 days, and is described as crampy and localised to the lower abdomen and pelvis.
 Imaging Findings: Ultrasound examination done on a Samsung RS80A ultrasound machine showed a bulky uterine body with hypoechoic endometrial collection (Figure 1). A heterogeneously hypoechoic lesion with internal echogenic debris was noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figure 2). The left ovary appears normal.
 
 Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) was performed using a 1.5 Tesla GE MR machine. T2-weighted sagittal and axial images of the MRI pelvis show the presence of the fundus and body of the uterus, which is oriented in the right lateral position in the pelvic cavity, with the absence of the cervix (Figure 3). However, the vagina is visualised. T1 hyperintense and T2 mildly hypointense endometrial collection is noted within the endometrial cavity (Figures 3 and 4). A well-defined T1 hyperintense and predominantly T2 hypointense (T2 shading sign) cystic lesion with a thin wall is noted in the right adnexa, arising from the right ovary (Figures 5 and 6). Minimal pelvic ascites is noted.

OriginalDescription:
Clinical History: A 54-year-old man known to have hypertension and type 2 diabetes experienced paraesthesia in his left hand and distal part of his left forearm for several months. On examination, multiple well-defined skin nodules were found, globally distributed, along with reduced sensation distally in the left upper limb.
 Imaging Findings: MRI of the brachial plexus with intravenous contrast showed multiple well-marginated lesions with heterogeneous signal in the infraclavicular part of the left brachial plexus, including the trunks and divisions (Figures 1, 2a, 2b, and 3), extending into the axilla and proximal parts of the upper arm, suggestive of neurofibromas.
 
 MRI of the spine and brain showed no abnormalities, including vestibular schwannoma, and the patient was diagnosed with schwannomatosis.
